content.text: Latency is the elapsed time from sending a request to receiving the last token of a response. For interactive applications it is often the most user-visible quality criterion: a technically correct but slow response damages the experience just as a fast but wrong one does. Latency has two components — time to first token (TTFT), which governs perceived responsiveness, and total generation time, which governs when the full answer is available. Prompt design affects both.
Trim system prompt, remove redundant context
Higher ceiling can allow longer completions
Set a tight ceiling; ask for concise output
Large increase — reasoning tokens add up
Disable for simple tasks; use streaming to hide it
Route simple tasks to a smaller model

import anthropic
import time

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def measure_latency(
    prompt: str,
    system: str = "",
    model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens: int = 300,
) -> dict:
    """
    Measure TTFT (time to first token) and total latency for a single call.
    Uses streaming to capture TTFT accurately.
    """
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    kwargs = {"model": model, "max_tokens": max_tokens, "messages": messages}
    if system:
        kwargs["system"] = system

    full_text = []
    ttft: float | None = None
    t_start = time.perf_counter()

    with client.messages.stream(**kwargs) as stream:
        for text in stream.text_stream:
            if ttft is None:
                ttft = time.perf_counter() - t_start
            full_text.append(text)

    total_time = time.perf_counter() - t_start
    output = "".join(full_text)

    return {
        "ttft_ms": round(ttft * 1000) if ttft is not None else None,
        "total_ms": round(total_time * 1000),
        "output_tokens": len(output.split()),  # approximate
        "chars_per_sec": round(len(output) / total_time),
    }


# Compare three prompt variants for the same task
BASE_QUESTION = "What is the capital of France?"

variants = [
    ("Default",    BASE_QUESTION),
    ("With CoT",   BASE_QUESTION + " Think step by step."),
    ("Concise",    BASE_QUESTION + " Answer in one word."),
]

for label, prompt in variants:
    stats = measure_latency(prompt)
    print(
        f"{label:12}: TTFT={stats['ttft_ms']}ms  "
        f"total={stats['total_ms']}ms  "
        f"~{stats['output_tokens']} tokens"
    )

A latency budget defines the maximum acceptable response time for a given interaction type. Different parts of a product have different budgets: a real-time autocomplete needs <200 ms TTFT while a background document summariser can tolerate 30 s total. Matching prompt complexity to the budget prevents over-engineering.

import anthropic
import time
import statistics

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def benchmark_prompt(
    prompt: str,
    system: str = "",
    model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens: int = 200,
    runs: int = 3,
) -> dict:
    """Run a prompt N times and return median / p95 latency statistics."""
    times = []
    for _ in range(runs):
        messages = [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
        kwargs = {"model": model, "max_tokens": max_tokens, "messages": messages}
        if system:
            kwargs["system"] = system
        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        client.messages.create(**kwargs)
        times.append((time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000)

    times_sorted = sorted(times)
    p95_index = int(0.95 * len(times_sorted))
    return {
        "median_ms": round(statistics.median(times)),
        "p95_ms": round(times_sorted[min(p95_index, len(times_sorted) - 1)]),
        "min_ms": round(min(times)),
        "max_ms": round(max(times)),
    }


# Measure the latency cost of a long vs. trimmed system prompt
LONG_SYSTEM = """
You are a helpful assistant for Acme Corp. Our company was founded in 1990.
We sell widgets, gadgets, and gizmos. We have 500 employees in 12 countries.
Our values are integrity, innovation, and inclusion. We use a ticket system
called TicketMaster (not the concert company). Customer SLAs are 24 hours for
normal tickets and 4 hours for urgent tickets. Always be polite and helpful.
Do not discuss competitors. Do not share internal pricing. Direct legal
questions to legal@acme.com. Always ask if there is anything else you can help with.
"""

SHORT_SYSTEM = (
    "You are Acme Corp support. Be concise and polite. "
    "Don't share internal pricing or discuss competitors."
)

USER_PROMPT = "How do I reset my password?"

for label, system in [("Long system prompt", LONG_SYSTEM), ("Trimmed system prompt", SHORT_SYSTEM)]:
    stats = benchmark_prompt(USER_PROMPT, system=system)
    print(f"{label}:")
    print(f"  Median: {stats['median_ms']}ms  P95: {stats['p95_ms']}ms  Range: {stats['min_ms']}–{stats['max_ms']}ms")
When the trade-off is worth it
Math, multi-step logic, classification with edge cases
Loses detail but keeps core answer
FAQ bots, real-time chat, mobile UIs

The biggest latency wins in prompt engineering come from output length, not input length. Cutting a 500-token response to 100 tokens by adding a conciseness instruction often reduces wall-clock time by 60–80%. Track both TTFT and total latency as separate metrics: a streaming UI where the first token arrives in 300 ms feels fast even if the full response takes 8 s. Set per-interaction-type budgets (autocomplete vs. document summary vs. background job) and tune prompt verbosity and model tier separately for each.
Why Latency Matters as a Prompt Quality Criterion

Latency is not just a server performance metric — it is a direct measure of how useful a prompt is for real-time applications. A technically correct but verbose prompt that produces a 10-second response is wrong for an autocomplete use case even if the answer quality is excellent. Prompt quality is always evaluated in the context of its use case: the same system prompt that is appropriate for a background document processing job is a latency disaster in a real-time chat interface.

Nielsen's research on human-computer interaction established that 100ms is the threshold for immediate feedback, 1 second is the threshold for maintaining the user's flow of thought, and 10 seconds is the upper limit for keeping the user's attention on the task. Modern LLM streaming can make even a 5-second total response feel fast if the first token arrives in under 500ms and words appear fluidly. Prompt design decisions — model tier, output length instructions, chain-of-thought — directly control where on this spectrum the product lands.

From a business perspective, latency compounds into throughput. A prompt that produces 300-token responses when 50-token responses are sufficient reduces API throughput by 6x for the same compute budget. At scale, this is the difference between serving 1 million requests per day and serving 6 million — the same hardware, the same model, just a tighter output length instruction.
Faster TTFT (time to first token) and shorter total response times directly correlate with higher user satisfaction scores in A/B tests on conversational AI products.

Shorter output tokens reduce compute cost proportionally. A 200-token response costs twice a 100-token response at the same input token count. Output length instructions are the highest-leverage cost lever.
Optimised prompts unlock use cases (autocomplete, real-time translation, live coaching) that are impossible with verbose prompts, expanding the product's addressable use case space.
Prompts that produce shorter, more constrained outputs have lower P99 latency variance. Unpredictable long responses cause tail latency spikes that SLA budgets cannot absorb.
Concise output instructions also improve answer quality by eliminating preamble, hedging, and padding that models add by default. Shorter and faster often means better.

Shorter outputs carry less nuance. A one-sentence answer to a complex question may be faster but incomplete. The optimal output length depends on the task and what 'quality' means for that interaction.
Routing simple tasks to Haiku/Flash reduces latency by 60-70% but loses capability on nuanced tasks. Incorrect routing amplifies errors at the cheapest tier.
Chain-of-thought adds 50-300% token overhead but improves accuracy on multi-step reasoning tasks. Disabling it for speed on those tasks trades reliability for latency.
Anthropic's prompt caching requires the cacheable prefix to be identical across calls. Dynamic system prompts (e.g., including current timestamp) break caching and eliminate TTFT savings.
Streaming shifts perceived vs. actual latency:
Streaming improves perceived speed but complicates error handling, post-processing, and structured output parsing. Non-streaming is simpler to implement for batch jobs.

LLM inference has two phases with different latency drivers. The prefill phase processes all input tokens in parallel and produces the first output token. Its duration scales roughly linearly with input token count because the attention computation is O(input_length^2) but modern hardware parallelises it. The decode phase generates output tokens autoregressively one at a time, each requiring a full forward pass plus a KV cache read. Total generation time scales linearly with output token count.

Where T_server is server queueing and network overhead (typically 50-200ms), k_prefill is the per-token prefill coefficient (typically 0.01-0.05ms per token for hosted APIs), k_decode is the per-output-token decode time (typically 10-30ms per token for hosted APIs), and N_input / N_output are the input and output token counts. This model shows that prompt size matters primarily for TTFT, while output length is the dominant driver of total latency.

Latency optimisation is essential when: the product is a real-time user-facing interface (chat, autocomplete, voice assistant, coding co-pilot), SLAs specify response time targets (e.g., P95 < 2s), the cost of API calls is significant and throughput needs to be maximised, or the product serves latency-sensitive integration points (e.g., inline suggestions in an IDE that appear as the user types).

Latency optimisation is less critical for: batch processing pipelines (document analysis, report generation, overnight jobs), developer tools where response quality outweighs speed, or offline evaluation frameworks. Spending engineering time on latency optimisation for a batch job that runs at 2am is wasteful — focus on quality instead.

A useful heuristic: if a human would perceive the response time during normal use, optimise for latency. If the job runs unattended, optimise for quality and cost instead. For most products, both categories exist — build per-interaction-type latency budgets and tune prompts separately for each tier.
Real-World Example: A/B Testing Prompt Latency

A customer support chatbot serves 50 000 conversations per day. Each conversation has an average of 5 turns. A team runs a systematic A/B test to measure the latency impact of prompt design choices without sacrificing answer quality.

import anthropic
import time
import statistics
import concurrent.futures
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


@dataclass
class LatencyStats:
    ttft_ms: list[float] = field(default_factory=list)
    total_ms: list[float] = field(default_factory=list)
    output_tokens: list[int] = field(default_factory=list)

    def summary(self) -> dict:
        def stats(vals):
            s = sorted(vals)
            return {
                "p50": round(statistics.median(s)),
                "p95": round(s[int(0.95 * len(s))] if len(s) > 1 else s[0]),
                "p99": round(s[int(0.99 * len(s))] if len(s) > 1 else s[0]),
                "mean": round(statistics.mean(s)),
            }
        return {
            "ttft_ms":      stats(self.ttft_ms),
            "total_ms":     stats(self.total_ms),
            "avg_tokens":   round(statistics.mean(self.output_tokens)),
        }


def measure_single(system: str, user: str, model: str, max_tokens: int) -> tuple:
    """Returns (ttft_ms, total_ms, output_token_count) for one streaming call."""
    ttft = None
    token_count = 0
    t_start = time.perf_counter()
    with client.messages.stream(
        model=model, max_tokens=max_tokens,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}]
    ) as stream:
        for text_chunk in stream.text_stream:
            if ttft is None:
                ttft = (time.perf_counter() - t_start) * 1000
            token_count += len(text_chunk.split())
    total = (time.perf_counter() - t_start) * 1000
    return ttft or 0, total, token_count


def ab_test(variants: dict, user_question: str, n_runs: int = 5) -> dict:
    """
    variants: {label: {system, model, max_tokens}}
    Returns per-variant LatencyStats summary.
    """
    results = {}
    for label, config in variants.items():
        stats = LatencyStats()
        for _ in range(n_runs):
            ttft, total, tokens = measure_single(
                system=config["system"],
                user=user_question,
                model=config["model"],
                max_tokens=config["max_tokens"],
            )
            stats.ttft_ms.append(ttft)
            stats.total_ms.append(total)
            stats.output_tokens.append(tokens)
        results[label] = stats.summary()
    return results


QUESTION = "How do I reset my account password if I've lost access to my email?"

variants = {
    "Verbose (Opus, no constraint)": {
        "system": ("You are a helpful customer support agent for Acme Corp. "
                   "Provide thorough, detailed responses. Always include all relevant "
                   "steps, caveats, and alternative options."),
        "model": "claude-opus-4-5",
        "max_tokens": 600,
    },
    "Concise (Haiku, constrained)": {
        "system": ("You are Acme Corp support. Answer in 3 steps maximum. "
                   "Be direct and concise. No preamble."),
        "model": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        "max_tokens": 150,
    },
    "Structured (Haiku, JSON output)": {
        "system": ("You are Acme Corp support. Respond with JSON: "
                   '{"steps": ["step1", "step2"], "note": "optional caveat"}. '
                   "3 steps maximum."),
        "model": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        "max_tokens": 200,
    },
}

print(f"A/B test: {n_runs} runs each\n")
n_runs = 3
results = ab_test(variants, QUESTION, n_runs=n_runs)

for label, stats in results.items():
    print(f"\n{label}:")
    print(f"  TTFT:  p50={stats['ttft_ms']['p50']}ms  p95={stats['ttft_ms']['p95']}ms")
    print(f"  Total: p50={stats['total_ms']['p50']}ms  p95={stats['total_ms']['p95']}ms")
    print(f"  Avg output tokens: {stats['avg_tokens']}")
# Expected: Haiku concise is ~4-10x faster total, comparable quality for this task.
Mistake 1: Using Chain-of-Thought for Simple Tasks

import anthropic, time
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# WRONG: Adding "think step by step" to a factual lookup
# that does not benefit from reasoning — just adds latency.

bad_prompt = "What is the capital of France? Think step by step and explain your reasoning."
good_prompt = "What is the capital of France? Answer in one word."

for label, prompt in [("With CoT (slow)", bad_prompt), ("Direct (fast)", good_prompt)]:
    t = time.perf_counter()
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens=200, temperature=0,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - t) * 1000
    tokens = resp.usage.output_tokens
    print(f"{label}: {elapsed_ms:.0f}ms, {tokens} output tokens")
    print(f"  Answer: {resp.content[0].text[:60].strip()}")
# CoT adds output tokens and latency with zero quality benefit for simple lookups.
Mistake 2: Not Setting max_tokens for Interactive APIs

import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# WRONG: No max_tokens constraint — the model may generate 1000+ tokens
# for a response that needs only 2 sentences, destroying latency budget.
resp_unconstrained = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    # max_tokens not set — defaults to model maximum (varies by model)
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain what a REST API is."}]
)
print(f"Unconstrained tokens: {resp_unconstrained.usage.output_tokens}")  # may be 400+

# CORRECT: Set max_tokens to a value appropriate for the use case.
# For a chat FAQ bot, 150 tokens (about 3 sentences) is usually sufficient.
resp_constrained = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=150,
    system="Answer concisely in 2-3 sentences.",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain what a REST API is."}]
)
print(f"Constrained tokens: {resp_constrained.usage.output_tokens}")     # ~60-100
print(resp_constrained.content[0].text)
Mistake 3: Measuring Total Time Instead of TTFT for Streaming UIs

import anthropic, time
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# WRONG for streaming UIs: measuring total response time.
# If you stream, the user sees the first token at TTFT, not at total completion.
# Optimising total time for a streaming UI optimises the wrong metric.

t = time.perf_counter()
resp = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens=300,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a short poem about Python."}]
)
total_wrong = (time.perf_counter() - t) * 1000
print(f"Non-streaming total: {total_wrong:.0f}ms (wrong metric for stream UIs)")

# CORRECT for streaming UIs: measure TTFT separately.
ttft = None
t = time.perf_counter()
with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens=300,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a short poem about Python."}]
) as stream:
    for chunk in stream.text_stream:
        if ttft is None:
            ttft = (time.perf_counter() - t) * 1000
            print(f"TTFT: {ttft:.0f}ms  ← user sees first word here")
        break  # stop after first chunk for demo
total_correct = (time.perf_counter() - t) * 1000
print(f"Total streaming time: {total_correct:.0f}ms (less relevant in streaming UIs)")
Explicitly constrain output length for every interactive API call. Default to the minimum that meets quality requirements, not the model maximum.
Include output length instructions in the system prompt:
'Answer in 2-3 sentences.' or '50 words maximum.' is more effective than relying on max_tokens alone because the model generates more focused content from the start.
Use streaming in all interactive UIs:
Streaming reduces perceived latency by 60-80% without changing actual computation. Never block a user-facing interface waiting for the full response.
Use a fast small model (Haiku, Flash) as the default and route to a larger model only when the task requires it. A simple classification router (also a small model call) pays for itself immediately.

Anthropic's prompt caching (cache_control: ephemeral) eliminates prefill cost for repeated system prompts. Ensure system prompts are stable (no dynamic timestamps or session IDs) to maximise hit rate.
Chain-of-thought prompting should be reserved for tasks that demonstrably benefit (math, multi-step reasoning, complex classification). Do not add it by default.
Track P95 and P99, not just median:
Latency distributions for LLMs are right-skewed. A 200ms median with a 10s P99 means 1% of users wait 10 seconds. Users notice tail latency even if median is fast.
Define separate TTFT and total latency targets for each interaction type (autocomplete: TTFT < 200ms; chat reply: total < 3s; background job: total < 60s) and test against them continuously.

At scale, the relationship between latency and throughput is governed by Little's Law: throughput = concurrency / average_latency. If average total latency is 2 seconds and you have 100 concurrent connections, throughput is 50 requests/second. Halving average latency (to 1 second) doubles throughput to 100 requests/second — without changing infrastructure costs.

For hosted APIs, concurrency is limited by rate limits (tokens per minute, requests per minute). A prompt that produces 100-token responses can sustain 2x the request rate of a 200-token response at the same tokens-per-minute limit. Output length optimisation is therefore a direct multiplier on effective API capacity.

# 1. Anthropic SDK: streaming and metrics
import anthropic, time
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Streaming with usage stats
with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens=100,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is 2+2?"}]
) as stream:
    text = stream.get_final_text()
    msg  = stream.get_final_message()
    print(f"Input tokens:  {msg.usage.input_tokens}")
    print(f"Output tokens: {msg.usage.output_tokens}")

# 2. Prompt caching — reduces TTFT for repeated system prompts
# Supported on Anthropic API for prompts > 1024 tokens
LONG_SYSTEM = "You are a helpful assistant. " + ("Rules. " * 300)  # >1024 tokens
resp = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens=50,
    system=[{"type": "text", "text": LONG_SYSTEM,
             "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}}],  # cache this prefix
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello."}]
)
# Second call: cache hit → TTFT drops significantly
resp2 = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens=50,
    system=[{"type": "text", "text": LONG_SYSTEM,
             "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}}],
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "How are you?"}]
)
print("Cache hit:", hasattr(resp2.usage, 'cache_read_input_tokens'))

# 3. LiteLLM: unified API with latency tracking across providers
# pip install litellm
# import litellm
# litellm.success_callback = ["langfuse"]  # log TTFT + total to observability tool
# resp = litellm.completion(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", messages=messages)

# 4. Langfuse / LangSmith: observability platforms for tracking latency per prompt version
# These capture TTFT, total latency, token counts, and costs per trace.
# Critical for A/B testing prompt variants in production.
Comparison with Other Prompt Quality Criteria
Use CoT for complex tasks only
Set length constraints in both prompt and max_tokens
Aligned — faster = fewer output tokens = cheaper
Cache grounding context to amortise TTFT cost

Latency optimisation introduces a specific reliability risk: if max_tokens is set too low, the model may be cut off mid-sentence or mid-JSON object, producing malformed output. This is particularly dangerous for structured output (JSON mode, XML) where a truncated response causes a parse error. Always add 20-30% headroom to max_tokens beyond your expected output length.

Prompt caching improves latency but creates a reliability dependency: if the cached prefix is evicted (Anthropic caches for 5 minutes for ephemeral type), the next call incurs full prefill latency. Systems with strict SLA requirements should design for cache miss latency, not cache hit latency, to avoid SLA breaches on cold cache.

Routing to smaller models for latency creates a latency-reliability trade-off. A simpler model that answers in 300ms may have a higher error rate on ambiguous inputs than a larger model that answers in 1.5s. Monitor error rates separately for each model tier in your routing architecture and implement fallback to larger models when confidence is low.
Latency has two components: TTFT (time to first token, driven by input length and server queue) and total time (driven primarily by output token count).
Output length is the highest-leverage latency lever: cutting response length from 300 to 100 tokens reduces total time by ~60% for the same input.
Streaming reduces perceived latency by 60-80% in user-facing interfaces — always use it for interactive applications.
Set explicit max_tokens for every interactive API call; never rely on model defaults in production.
Chain-of-thought adds 50-300% token overhead — use it only for tasks that demonstrably require multi-step reasoning.
Smaller model tiers (Haiku, Flash) are 40-70% faster and cheaper; build a routing layer that sends simple tasks to the fast tier.
Prompt caching eliminates prefill cost for stable system prompts — requires the cacheable prefix to be identical across calls.
Track TTFT and total latency separately as distinct metrics; a streaming UI should optimise TTFT, a batch job should optimise total.
P95/P99 tail latency matters more than median for user-facing products — a slow 1% still causes noticeable degradation.
